ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Sambal Tumis; closest defensible match is chili sauce food product (FOODON_03301710). Sambal Tumis is a cooked, oil-broken chili paste distinct from generic chili sauce.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Chilli sauce) is the closest defensible match. Sambal Tumis is a traditional slow-cooked chili paste with belacan and aromatics, not a commercial chilli sauce; approximate match only.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Sambal Tumis or a sufficiently close analogue in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Sambal Tumis as an accompaniment is approximately 25 g (about 1 heaped tablespoon), as used alongside Nasi Lemak or as a base condiment for dishes such as Sambal Udang.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional Composition of Malaysian Foods (IMR Malaysia MyFCD) — Sambal / Chili-based condiment entries; supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia and USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level cross-check
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia; FAO/INFOODS; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for Sambal Tumis as a composite dish. Values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD sambal and chili paste entries, cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA chili condiment data and USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data for dried chili, shallot, garlic, and palm oil. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this cross-check and have been retained with minor adjustments. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 3.5 g per 100 g based on palm oil fraction; monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not available in the approved sources for this composite preparation. Cholesterol set to -1 as belacan contribution is minor and not separately reported. Water content set to -1 as the pecah minyak cooking process drives off most moisture but exact residual water is not reported in approved sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed chili condiment with added sugar
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Sambal Tumis exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the dominant macronutrient profile being fat and fibre-rich chili with moderate added palm sugar; (2) closest analogous entries for mixed chili-oil condiments with added sugar in the University of Sydney GI Database indicating low GI in the range 40–50; (3) the pecah minyak cooking process concentrates fat and fibre, which attenuates glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 4.6 g) / 100 = 2.1. Available carbohydrate per serving = (18.5 − 4.5) g per 100 g × 0.25 = 3.5 g net carb per 25 g serving; GL = (45 × 3.5) / 100 = 1.6, rounded to 2.1 using total carbohydrate basis (18.5 × 0.25 = 4.625 g). Confidence: 0.7 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (850 mg per 100 g; approximately 213 mg per 25 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit Sambal Tumis to no more than one small serving (≤15 g) per meal, as even a standard 25 g serving contributes approximately 213 mg sodium — roughly 9–14% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate added sugar (palm sugar; 12.5 g sugar per 100 g) and low glycemic index (GI ~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~45) and high fat and fibre content of Sambal Tumis attenuate postprandial glucose response; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion sizes carefully as the added palm sugar contributes approximately 3.1 g of sugar per standard 25 g serving, and cumulative intake across a meal can be significant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (22.5 g per 100 g) with estimated saturated fat (~3.5 g per 100 g from palm oil) and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambal Tumis is high in total fat and sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict serving size to ≤15 g per meal and choose preparations using less palm oil or substitute with a heart-healthier oil to reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Capsaicin from dried red chilies has documented anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Capsaicin in Sambal Tumis may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefit; however, the saturated fat from palm oil and sodium from belacan should be moderated in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Tumis is a foundational cooked chili condiment central to Malay and Indonesian cuisine, widely consumed across Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. The name derives from 'sambal' (chili-based condiment) and 'tumis' (to stir-fry or sauté in oil). Unlike raw Sambal Belacan, Sambal Tumis is slow-cooked in oil until the mixture reaches the 'pecah minyak' (oil-breaking) stage, at which point the water has fully evaporated, the flavors have intensified, and the oil separates visibly from the paste — a hallmark of a properly made Sambal Tumis. The paste is made by blending dried red chilies, shallots, garlic, and ginger, then frying this blend with belacan (fermented shrimp paste) in oil. It is seasoned with tamarind juice for sourness, palm sugar for sweetness, and salt, producing a rich, dark red, savory-sweet-sour sauce with a deeply aromatic, slightly smoky character. The texture is thick and oily, with a concentrated chili heat balanced by the sweetness of palm sugar. Sambal Tumis serves as both a standalone accompaniment — most famously as the essential chili component of Nasi Lemak — and as a cooking base for dishes such as Sambal Udang (prawn sambal), Sambal Sotong (squid sambal), and Sambal Telur (egg sambal). Nutritionally, Sambal Tumis is energy-dense at approximately 285 kcal per 100 g, driven primarily by its high fat content (~22.5 g per 100 g, largely from cooking oil and palm oil). It contains moderate carbohydrates (~18.5 g per 100 g) with a notable sugar component (~12.5 g per 100 g) from palm sugar, and a meaningful dietary fibre contribution (~4.5 g per 100 g) from the chili and aromatics. Sodium is high at 850 mg per 100 g due to belacan and salt. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), as the high fat and fibre content attenuates glycemic response despite the added sugar. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, and portion control is recommended. The low GI makes it relatively suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in small portions. Vitamin A (120 mcg RAE per 100 g) and Vitamin C (15 mg per 100 g) are contributed by the dried red chilies.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Sambal Tumis as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD sambal and chili paste entries, cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with this cross-check. GI is an analogous estimate. Saturated fat is estimated from the palm oil fraction. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g per 100 g) is estimated from the palm oil fraction of the cooking oil used; no direct approved-source measurement for this composite preparation is available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, cholesterol, and moisture data for Sambal Tumis as a composite preparation are not separately reported in IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Sambal Tumis contains no animal-derived vitamin D sources; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data for chili, shallot, garlic, and palm oil. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Sambal Tumis exists. 5. The input source cited ('MyHealth Ministry of Health Malaysia') is not on the approved source list; it has been replaced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, and USDA FoodData Central as the authoritative bases. 6. Multilingual names for Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as this dish is widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia across ethnic communities; Indonesian is included as Sambal Tumis is also a staple Indonesian preparation.
